questions.text: I think of structure as something that should only be added if the work clearly demands it.
I prefer straightforward conversations where everything is on the table.
I feel that personal boundaries should be flexible when someone truly needs my help.
I would describe my influence style as collaborative.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering becomes more pressing the more clearly I see the connection between what I can do and what the person needs.
My startle response is pronounced — unexpected sounds or events jolt me intensely.
I have found that I am most useful in emotionally charged situations when I do not allow myself to be drawn into the emotion.
I feel that keeping a windfall for myself when others are in need would be wrong.
I am comfortable exercising authority even when it creates tension.
I am easily swept up in the emotional energy of a crowd or a room.
I tend to measure my week by how much I advanced my own objectives.
I do not usually notice when someone answers a question for another person to control the narrative.
I prefer to be the single point of contact for important communications.
I coordinate complex work through conversations and shared intuition rather than frameworks.
I find that sticking to a proven methodology gives me confidence in uncertain situations.
I find that I naturally gravitate toward opportunities that advance my own position.
I feel that I function best when I regularly attend to my own needs first.
I am quick to notice when someone's status in a group has shifted.
I find satisfaction in perfecting a process until it runs smoothly every time.
I maintain an inventory of supplies so I can reorder before running out.
I think that the ability to remain emotionally neutral during a crisis is one of the most underappreciated forms of service.
I consider it a compliment when people describe me as commanding.
I sense when a team member is withdrawing their investment in a project through subtle disengagement.
I believe that the moral concern I feel for those who are suffering is the foundation upon which all my other values rest.
I enjoy the challenge of finding common ground with skeptics through mutual respect.
I find that I read interpersonal dynamics like a language I have always been fluent in.
I recover quickly from emotional events and return to my baseline without much effort.
I find that my body treats emotional boundaries the way it treats physical boundaries — crossing one produces a visceral reaction.
I focus fully on the current step and trust that the next step will become clear.
I experience the stress of a tight schedule as a practical reality rather than a source of internal turmoil.
I generally do not analyze why someone chose a particular word when a simpler one would do.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is the moral claim that should never be subordinated to efficiency or expediency.
I tend to define a good life as one spent in generous contribution to others.
I find that my ability to process painful information without becoming emotionally destabilized is crucial to my effectiveness in high-stakes environments.
I notice that I calibrate my effort to match the group's established rhythm.
I find that I can be in a noisy, chaotic environment without feeling overstimulated.
